title: Complete Python Mastery
description: Master Python from scratch with 30 interactive, follow-along classes.

classes.title: Class 1: Building Your Lab
classes.description: Setting up the professional tools you need to code.

content.text:
Imagine you want to cook a 5-star meal. You have the ingredients (Code), but you need a proper kitchen (VS Code) and a stove (Python Interpreter) to cook them.

Today, we are building your professional kitchen.

First, we need the machine that actually runs your code. This is **Python** itself.

1. Go to **python.org**.
2. Download the latest version for Windows.
3. **CRITICAL**: When installing, check the box that says **'Add Python to PATH'**.

Now we need a place to write our recipes. **VS Code** is the world's most popular code editor.

1. Go to **code.visualstudio.com**.
2. Download and install it.
3. Open it up - this is your new home!

VS Code is smart, but we can make it genius. We need the **Python Extension**.

1. In VS Code, click the **Extensions** icon (looks like blocks on the left).
2. Search for **'Python'** (by Microsoft).
3. Click **Install**.

This gives you color-coding and error checking!

Let's test our kitchen.

1. In VS Code, create a new file named `test.py`.
2. Write this line: `print('Systems Online')`
3. **Right-click** anywhere in the editor and select **'Run Python File in Terminal'**.

You should see 'Systems Online' appear at the bottom!

content.question: What happens if you forget to install the Python Extension?
options: VS Code won't understand Python code well
content.answer: VS Code won't understand Python code well

classes.title: Class 2: The Universal Language
classes.description: Why Python differs from every other language.

content.text:
Why do millions love Python? Because it speaks **Human**, not **Robot**.

Other languages demand complex grammar (syntax). Python just wants to know what you want to do. It's the closest thing to simply talking to your computer.

Look at how we print text in **Java**:
`System.out.println("Hello");`

Now look at **Python**:
`print("Hello")`

See the difference? Python removes the clutter so you can focus on the logic.

Imagine a CEO. They don't want to know how the electricity in the office works; they just want the company to grow. 

Python is a **High-Level** language. It handles the 'electricity' (memory and CPU details) for you, so you can focus on building the 'company' (your app).

You're in good company. **NASA** uses Python to analyze stars. **Netflix** uses it to recommend movies. **Google** was built on it.

By learning this, you are learning the tool of the giants.

content.question: Python is considered a 'High-Level' language. What does that mean?
options: It's closer to human language than machine code
content.answer: It's closer to human language than machine code

content.question: What serves as the 'Stove' in our kitchen analogy?

classes.title: Class 3: The Megaphone (Output)
classes.description: Learning how to make your computer speak using print().

content.text:
Every programmer starts with 'Hello World'. Why? Because the most basic thing a computer can do is tell you what it's thinking.

In Python, we use the `print()` function. Think of it as a **Megaphone**. Whatever you put inside the parentheses `()` gets shouted out to your screen.
Type exactly this: `print("Hello Python")` and hit Run. Note the double quotes `" "`—they tell Python this is text, not a command.
content.hint: Don't forget the quotes and parentheses!

content.text:
What if you want to shout on two different lines? 

You can use `\n`. It stands for **New Line**.

Example: `print("Hello\nWorld")`
Print `Line 1` and `Line 2` on two separate lines using a single `print()` command.

content.question: What happens if you forget the parentheses in print "Hello"?

classes.title: Class 4: Labelled Boxes (Variables)
classes.description: How to store information so you can use it later.

content.text:
Imagine you are moving houses. If you throw everything into one big pile, you'll never find your socks.

You use **Boxes with Labels**. 

In Python, a **Variable** is a box. The **Name** is the label on the box, and the **Value** is what's inside. 

Example: `score = 10` means 'Put the number 10 into a box named score'.
Create a variable named `city` and set it equal to `"New York"`. Then `print(city)`.

In Python, we use **snake_case** for variables. 

Use all lowercase and underscores for spaces.
- `user_age` (Good)
- `UserAge` (Avoid)
- `user age` (Error - no spaces allowed!)

content.question: Which of these is a valid Python variable name?

content.text:
You can change what's inside a box anytime. 

`x = 5` then `x = 10` means the 5 is thrown away and replaced by 10.
Create `health = 100`, then on the next line update `health` to be `80`. Finally, print `health`.
content.hint: Just write health = 80 before the print!

classes.title: Class 5: The Digital Calculator
classes.description: Working with Integers, Floats, and arithmetic.

content.text:
In Python, numbers come in two flavors:

1. **Integers (int)**: Whole numbers like `5` or `-2`.
2. **Floats**: Decimals like `3.14`.

You can use `+`, `-`, `*` (multiply), and `/` (divide).
Multiply `10` by `5` and store it in `result`. Then `print(result)`.

The **Modulo** operator `%` gives you the REMAINDER of a division.

Example: `10 % 3` is `1` (because 3 goes into 10 three times with 1 left over).

This is super useful for checking if a number is Even or Odd!

In Python, `**` is used for 'Power of'. 

Calculate 2 to the power of 3 (2³) and print it.

content.question: What is the result of 10 / 2 in Python?

classes.title: Class 6: Beads on a String
classes.description: Manipulating text and formatting with f-strings.

content.text:
Text in Python is called a **String**. Think of it as beads on a string. 

The modern way to format text is using **f-strings**. 

Example: `print(f"My name is {name}")`
Use an f-string to print: `Hello, Alice`. (Variable `user` is provided).

Strings have built-in skills called **Methods**.
- `.upper()` makes text LOUD.
- `.lower()` makes text quiet.
- `len()` tells you how many characters (beads) are in the string.
Print the variable `msg` in all UPPERCASE.

content.question: What is len('Python')?

classes.title: Class 7: Talking Back (Input)
classes.description: Getting information from the user with input().

content.text: Programs that listen are much more useful. We use the `input()` function to ask the user a question.
What function do we use to get text from a user?

**CRITICAL**: `input()` always treats everything as Text (String). 

If you ask for an age and the user types `25`, Python sees `"25"`. You can't do math with text!

To turn text into a number, we use `int()` (for whole numbers) or `float()` (for decimals).

Example: `age = int(input("Enter age: "))`
Ask the user for their age using `input("Age: ")`, convert it to an integer, and add `1` to it. Then print the result.
content.hint: Use input("Age: ") in the first line.

classes.title: Class 8: The Power Switch (Booleans)
classes.description: Yes/No logic in programming.

content.text:
Everything in logic boils down to **True** or **False**. This is called a **Boolean**.

You create them with comparisons like `>` (greater than) or `==` (is equal to).

- `==` (Equal to)
- `!=` (NOT equal to)
- `>` (Greater than)
- `<` (Less than)
- `>=` (Greater or equal)
- `<=` (Less or equal)

content.question: What is the result of '10 != 10'?

content.text: Compare if `password` is equal to `"1234"` and print the True/False result.

classes.title: Class 9: Traffic Lights (Logic Gates)
classes.description: Combining conditions with AND, OR, and NOT.

content.text:
- **AND**: Both must be True.
- **OR**: At least one must be True.
- **NOT**: Flipped value.

Imagine a nightclub. To get in, you need `has_ticket` AND `is_over_18`.

If one is False, you stay outside!

content.question: True AND False equals...?

content.text: Check if `age` is 18+ OR `has_permission` is True. Print the result.
content.answer:
age = 16
has_permission = True
print(age >= 18 or has_permission)

classes.title: Class 10: The Big 'IF'
classes.description: Making decisions with the if statement.

content.text:
Code usually runs like a straight road. An **if** statement is a fork in the road.

`if age > 18:` means 'If this is true, do the code below it'.

Python is very picky. You MUST put a colon `:` at the end of the if line, and you MUST indent the code inside it (usually 4 spaces).

If it's not indented, Python doesn't think it's part of the decision!
Complete the if statement to print 'Access Granted' if `passcode` is `777`.

content.question: What happens if you don't indent code inside an if statement?

classes.title: Class 11: Plan B (Else & Elif)
classes.description: Handling alternative paths and multiple choices.

content.text:
An `if` statement only runs if the condition is True. What if it's False? 

We use **else** for a 'default' path and **elif** (else-if) to check multiple specific rules.
Complete the logic: If `score` is 90+ print 'A', otherwise (else) print 'B'.
content.hint: Just print 'B' inside the else block!

content.text:
You can have as many `elif` statements as you want between the `if` and the `else`.

Python checks them in order and stops at the first one that is True.
If `temp` is above 30 print 'Hot', else if (`elif`) `temp` is above 20 print 'Warm', otherwise print 'Cold'.
content.hint: Fill in the elif condition: temp > 20

classes.title: Class 12: Logic inside Logic (Nested)
classes.description: Nesting conditions within conditions.

content.text:
Sometimes you need to clear one hurdle before you check the next. 

Example: `if has_ticket:` then inside that `if is_front_row:`.

Be careful! If you nest too deep (like 5 or 6 times), your code becomes hard to read. This is often called 'Spaghetti Code'.

Always try to keep your logic as flat as possible.

content.question: How does Python know a line of code is 'inside' an if statement?

content.text: Check if `is_logged_in` is True, and INSIDE that, check if `is_admin` is True. If both are True, print `"Welcome Boss"`.
content.hint: Add the second if condition: if is_admin:

classes.title: Class 13: Groundhog Day (While Loops)
classes.description: Repeating code until a condition is no longer met.

content.text:
Computers are great at boring work. A **while** loop repeats code as long as a condition is True. 

Careful: If the condition never becomes False, your program will loop forever!
Finish the while loop to print `count` while it is greater than `0`. (Don't forget to subtract 1 each time!).
content.answer:
count = 3
while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count = count - 1

content.text:
Most loops need a **counter** (like our variable `count`). 

If you forget to change the counter inside the loop, the condition stays True forever. This is an **Infinite Loop**!

content.question: If a while loop condition is False the very first time, how many times does the code inside run?

classes.title: Class 14: The Conveyor Belt (For Loops)
classes.description: Iterating over a range or a collection of items.

content.text:
A **for** loop is used when you know exactly how many times you want to repeat, or if you want to look at every item in a list.

`for i in range(5):` means 'Do this 5 times'.

The `range()` function is more powerful than you think:
- `range(5)` -> 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
- `range(1, 5)` -> 1, 2, 3, 4
- `range(0, 10, 2)` -> 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 (Steps by 2!)

content.question: How many times will 'for i in range(10):' run?

content.text: Use a `for` loop and `range()` to print all EVEN numbers from `0` up to (but not including) `10`.
content.hint: Use 2 as the third number in range!

classes.title: Class 15: Breaking the Cycle
classes.description: Controlling loops with break and continue.

content.text:
- **break**: Stops the loop completely and exits.
- **continue**: Skips the rest of the current turn and goes to the next one.

content.question: Which command stops a loop immediately?

content.text: In a loop from 1 to 10, use `break` if the number is `5`. Print each number.
content.hint: Write break inside the if block!

classes.title: Class 16: The Shopping List (Lists)
classes.description: Storing multiple items in a single variable.

content.text:
Imagine you are making a grocery app. Storing 50 items in 50 variables like `item1`, `item2`... is a nightmare.

We need a way to store a single collection of many items. In Python, we use **Lists**.

Lists are created using square brackets `[ ]` and commas.

Example: `fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]`
Create a list named `colors` containing `"red"`, `"green"`, and `"blue"`. Then print the whole list.
content.hint: Use square brackets and quotes for each color!

content.text:
Computers start counting at **0**, not 1.

In our list `["A", "B", "C"]`:
- Index 0 is "A"
- Index 1 is "B"
- Index 2 is "C"

content.question: If animals = ["cat", "dog", "bird"], what is animals[1]?

content.text: Print only the FIRST item from the provided list `names`.
content.hint: Use names[0] to get the first item!

classes.title: Class 17: List Workshop (Methods)
classes.description: Adding, removing, and sorting items in a list.

content.text:
Lists are 'mutable', which means you can change them after they are created.

To add an item to the end, use `.append()`.

Example: `cart.append("milk")`
Add `"grapes"` to the end of the `fruits` list using `.append()`, then print the list.
content.hint: Use fruits.append("grapes") before the print line.

content.text:
You can remove items too:
- `.pop(0)` removes by index (removes item at index 0).
- `.remove("item")` removes by the actual name of the item.

content.question: Which method do you use if you know the POSITION (index) of the item you want to delete?

content.text:
Want things in alphabetical or numerical order? Use `.sort()`.

Example: `numbers.sort()`
Sort the `scores` list and then print it.

classes.title: Class 18: The Digital Wallet (Dictionaries)
classes.description: Using Key-Value pairs to store structured data.

content.text:
Lists are great for sequences, but for 'real world objects', we need labels.

Think of a **Dictionary**. You look up a **Key** (Word) to find its **Value** (Definition).

In Python: `user = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}`
Create a dictionary named `car` with keys `"brand"` (value "Tesla") and `"model"` (value "S"). Then print it.
content.hint: Use curly braces { } and colons between keys and values!

content.text:
Instead of using numbers (indexes), you use the label (key).

Example: `print(user["name"])` gives you "Alice".
Print ONLY the `age` from the `user` dictionary.
content.hint: Use square brackets with the key name: user["age"]

content.question: What symbols are used to define a Dictionary in Python?

classes.title: Class 19: The Locked Safe (Tuples & Sets)
classes.description: Learning about unchangeable lists and unique collections.

content.text:
Sometimes you want a list that **cannot** be changed. 

Example: The coordinates of a city or the RGB values of a color. We use **Tuples** for this. They use parentheses `( )` instead of square brackets.
Create a tuple named `point` with values `10` and `20`. Then print it.

content.question: What happens if you try to use .append() on a Tuple?

content.text:
A **Set** is a collection where every item **must be unique**. If you try to add "Apple" twice, it only keeps one.

Sets are great for removing duplicates from a list.

classes.title: Class 20: Cutting the Cake (Slicing)
classes.description: Mastering indexing and extracting parts of data.

content.text:
You already know `list[0]` gets one item. But what if you want the first three?

We use **Slicing**: `list[start : stop]`. 

Note: The `stop` index is NOT included!
Get the first 3 letters of the string `word` using `[:3]` and print them.

You can add a third number for the **Step**. 

`[::-1]` is a famous Python shortcut that reverses a string or list!
Reverse the string `secret` using slicing and print the result.

content.question: In Python, what index represents the VERY LAST item in a list?

classes.title: Class 21: The Robot Command (Functions)
classes.description: Writing reusable blocks of code to stay efficient.

content.text:
The golden rule of programming is: **If you write the same code twice, you're doing it wrong.**

We use **Functions** to wrap code into a named command that we can call whenever we want.

- **Defining**: Teaching the robot a new trick (`def trick():`)
- **Calling**: Telling the robot to DO the trick (`trick()`)
Define a function named `greet` that prints `"Hello!"`. Then call it on the next line.

Functions become powerful when you give them data to work with. These are called **Parameters**.

Example: `def greet(name):`
Update the function to take a `name` and print `f"Hello {name}"`. Then call it with `"Python"`.
content.hint: Use an f-string inside the print!

content.question: Why do professionals use functions?
options: To reuse code and keep it organized
content.answer: To reuse code and keep it organized

classes.title: Class 22: Where do I live? (Scope)
classes.description: Understanding Local vs Global variables.

content.text:
Variables have 'homes'. 

- **Global Variables**: Defined outside functions. Everyone can see them.
- **Local Variables**: Defined inside a function. They 'die' when the function finishes.

content.question: If I create 'x = 5' inside a function, can I print it outside the function?

classes.title: Class 23: The Safety Net (Try/Except)
classes.description: Preventing your program from crashing on errors.

content.text:
Programs fail. Maybe a user types a word instead of a number, or tries to divide by zero.

Professional code uses **Try / Except** to catch errors gracefully instead of crashing.
Wrap the code in a `try` block, and in the `except` block, print `"Error!"`.
content.hint: Just indent a print statement under except!

classes.title: Class 24: Standing on Giants (Modules)
classes.description: Using libraries to expand Python's capabilities.

content.text:
Python has thousands of 'Modules' (Toolboxes) ready for you. 

Need math? `import math`. 
Need random numbers? `import random`.
Import `math` and print the square root of 16 using `math.sqrt(16)`.
content.hint: Just import math at the top!

content.text: Import `random` and print a random integer between 1 and 10 using `random.randint(1, 10)`.

classes.title: Class 25: The Digital Notebook (Files)
classes.description: Reading and writing data to text files.

content.text:
When your program closes, all variables vanish. To keep data, we save it to a file.

The modern way to do this is using the `with` keyword.
Write `"Hello File"` to a file named `note.txt`.
content.answer:
with open("note.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello File")

content.question: What does the 'w' stand for in open('file.txt', 'w')?

classes.title: Class 26: The Blueprints (Classes & OOP)
classes.description: Learning how to model real-world objects in code.

content.text:
Imagine you are building a game with 100 cars. You don't want to create 100 sets of variables. 

You create a **Class** (a Blueprint) and use it to create **Objects** (the actual cars).
Define an empty class named `User` using the `pass` keyword.
When you create an object, you usually want to give it data immediately (like a name). We use the `__init__` method for this.
Inside the class, define `__init__` to set `self.name`.

content.question: What does 'self' represent in a Python class?

classes.title: Class 27: The Family Tree (Inheritance)
classes.description: Sharing logic between different classes.

content.text: If you have a `Vehicle` class, you don't need to rewrite the 'Drive' logic for `Car` and `Truck`. They can **Inherit** those traits.
Make class `Dog` inherit from `Animal`.
content.hint: Put Animal inside the parentheses of Dog!

classes.title: Class 28: The Speed Loop (Comprehensions)
classes.description: Writing powerful loops in a single line of code.

content.text:
Instead of writing 4 lines for a loop, you can do it in 1. This is called a **List Comprehension**.

Example: `[x*2 for x in numbers]`
Create a list of numbers from 0 to 4 using a list comprehension.
content.answer:
nums = [x for x in range(5)]
print(nums)

classes.title: Class 29: Professional Setup (VENV)
classes.description: Managing packages and isolated environments.

content.text: In the real world, different projects need different versions of libraries. We use **Virtual Environments (venv)** to keep them separate.

content.question: What is the standard tool used to install Python packages?

classes.title: Class 30: Graduation (The Road Ahead)
classes.description: Final project overview and continuing your journey.

content.text:
Congratulations! You've gone from `print()` to Object-Oriented Programming and File I/O.

This is just the beginning. The world of Web Development (Django/Flask), Data Science (Pandas), and AI (PyTorch) is now open to you.
Combine your knowledge: Create a function `calc` that returns `a * b`. Then use it to print `5 * 5`.

You have completed the Complete Python Mastery course. 

**Next Steps:**
- Build a personal project.
- Contribute to Open Source.
- Keep learning every day.
